flags.note: נֶפֶשׁ — "soul" (Greek-influenced: non-material self) vs. "life" (the whole living person) vs. "self" (the person as subject). Hebrew nefesh is embodied, not a separate immaterial substance.

48.struct: contains a main verb; ⚑ theologically contested vocabulary

tokens.range: enclosure (especially for flocks)
a fortification, castle, or fortified city; figuratively, a defender
properly, a dissolution; concretely, a ruin, or (abstractly) consternation
to break out (in many applications, direct and indirect, literal and figurative)
to put (used in a great variety of applications, literal, figurative, inferentially, and elliptically)
properly, to spin round (under the influence of any violent emotion), i.e. usually rejoice, or (as cringing) fear
a day (as the warm hours), whether literal (from sunrise to sunset, or from one sunset to the next), or figurative (a space of time defined by an associated term), (often used adverb)
rightness (abstractly), subjectively (rectitude), objectively (justice), morally (virtue) or figuratively (prosperity)
to be high actively, to rise or raise (in various applications, literally or figuratively)
an appellation, as amark or memorial of individuality; by implication honor, authority, character

flags.note: צְדָקָה — "righteousness" (moral quality) vs. "justice" (social-legal action). OT prophets use this for God's vindicating action for the oppressed; "righteousness" alone misses the social dimension.

a sacred place or thing; rarely abstract, sanctity
grease, especially liquid (as from the olive, often perfumed); figuratively, richness

flags.note: עוֹלָם — "eternal" / "everlasting" / "age-long." Like Greek αἰώνιος, debate centers on whether this is absolute timelessness or the span of an era.

flags.note: יהוה — The divine name (YHWH). LORD (small caps) hides the personal name. Yahweh/Jehovah are reconstructions of the consonants. The MKT's choice shapes the reader's sense of intimacy with God.
